text: The surgeon identifies the hiatal hernia and begins dissection at the right crus of the diaphragm, focusing on the dorsal aspects of the hernia. The dissection progresses towards the ventral aspects, addressing dense adhesions at the left crus near the angle of His. The distal esophagus is dissected, revealing the size of the hernia. The surgeon carefully removes adhesions on the right side, ensuring the vagal nerve is not damaged, and the left crus becomes visible.

On the left side, the surgeon removes the remaining adhesions at the angle of His to fully mobilize the distal esophagus and reduce the hernia. A 32-gauge gastric tube is visible in place. The surgeon places two to three non-absorbable sutures between the crura of the diaphragm, ensuring adequate tissue is included to prevent muscle tearing. The second stitch is positioned approximately one centimeter dorsally from the first.

The surgeon closes the gap and prepares to introduce a synthetic bioabsorbable mesh through a twelve millimeter trocar. The mesh is wrapped around the distal esophagus, with an opening for the esophagus and not closed ventrally. It is tucked underneath surrounding tissue to sit on the diaphragm's crura. The mesh is fixed by attaching the right side to the ventral part of the right cruse and similarly on the left side, using one stitch per side. A final stitch is placed dorsally to the right cruse to complete the fixation.

The surgeon places a stay suture on the mobilized fundus and pulls it over to the right side behind the distal esophagus using a gold finger instrument. Despite the nut of the stay suture opening, the suture is successfully pulled over. A 270-degree fundoplication is performed by attaching the fundus to the ventral lateral aspect of the distal esophagus with three non-resorbable stitches on the right side, each placed about one centimeter apart. The same procedure is repeated on the left side with three additional stitches.
The surgeon ties the last knot ventrally and places an additional stitch posteriorly to the dorsal aspect of the right cruse, completing the operation.
